spans.content: Supplementary Materials for
Constrained minimal-interface structures in polycrystalline copper with
extremely fine grains
X. Y. Li*, Z. H. Jin, X. Zhou, K. Lu*
*Corresponding author. Email: xyli@imr.ac.cn (X.Y.L.); lu@imr.ac.cn (K.L.)
Published 13 November 2020, Science 370, 831 (2020)
DOI: 10.1126/science.abe1267
This PDF file includes:
Materials and Methods
Supplementary Text
Figs. S1 to S7
Tables S1 to S3
References
Correction: In the updated file, the authors corrected three in-text citations and updated Table S3. On page
5, two in-text citation typos have been corrected (correcting three reference numbers) so that the citations
refer to the intended references. The reference list itself was not affected. The simplified caption originally
included for supplementary Table S3 has been replaced by a detailed caption, and the fourth column of
Table S3 was changed from (C11-C22)/2 to the anisotropy parameter. None of the underlying data was
changed.
Corrected 30 November 2020; see below
Science
AAS
science.sciencemag.org/content/370/6518/831/suppl/DC1
Materials and Methods
1. Sample preparation
Polycrystalline pure Cu samples with extremely fine grains were prepared using a
two-step cryogenic plastic deformation process. Cu bars with a purity of
were
annealed at
4 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }
for
^ { 2 \mathrm { h } }
to form fully recrystallized structures with coarse grains. In the
first step, the coarse-grained Cu bar was processed using a surface mechanical grinding
treatment (SMGT (35)) in liquid nitrogen with processing parameters listed in Table S1.
For each sample, the SMGT process was repeated several times in order to achieve a
large accumulative plastic strain in the surface layer (an estimated strain larger than 100).
The strain rates in the top surface layer was estimated to be
. Due to formation of a
gradient nanograined surface layer that is effective in suppressing strain localization,
plastic deformation is rather uniform in the sample surface with a surface roughness Ra≈
0 . 3 \mu \mathrm { m }
. No crack and contamination were identified in the surface layer of the SMGT
sample. In the top surface layer of
2 0 \mu \mathrm { m }
thick of the Cu rod, highly stable nanograins are
formed. This layer is then removed from the rod and processed further in the second step.
In the second step, the foil sample cut from the SMGT Cu rod was electropolished in
an electrolyte (phosphoric acid: alcohol: deionized water=1:1:2) for removing possible
contamination. Then the foil was deformed by using a high pressure torsion (HPT)
facility on a rectangular flat die with a processing pressure up to
1 0 \mathrm { G P a }
. The number of
the revolving cycles is 5 with a rate of about
3 0 \mathrm { r p m }
. The samples and dies were
immersed in liquid nitrogen during the entire HPT deformation process. The schematic of
the HPT processing is displayed in Fig. S1. After the treatment, grains were refined into
the nanometer scale in the as-HPT sample. The sample contains extremely-fine grained
(below
1 0 \mathrm { n m }
) domains of several to ten microns in size embedded in regions with larger
grains (averagely about
2 5 \mathrm { n m }
in size).
2. Structure characterization
Structural characterization of the as-prepared samples and the annealed samples
were performed on a scanning electron microscope (SEM, FEI Verios 460) and
transmission electron microscopes (TEM, FEI Talos F200X, FEI Titan ChemisSTEM,
FEI Titan Themis), respectively. The sample preparation for SEM and TEM observations
follows the ordinary route of cutting, mechanical polishing, and final electro-chemical
polishing or thinning. The final finishing was accomplished by electro-chemical method
using an electrolyte consisting of
alcohol,
phosphorus acid and
de-ionized
water at
0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }
3. Hardness measurement
For the nanograined samples with average grain sizes of
2 5 \mathrm { n m }
and
1 0 \mathrm { n m }
nanohardness tests were performed on a Hysitron Tribo-indenter with the measurement
conditions of a preset load of
3 0 0 0 \mu \mathrm { N } _ { \mathrm { \Omega } }
, a loading and unloading rate of
6 0 0 \mu \mathrm { N } / \mathrm { s }
, and a
holding time of 2 seconds. Typical load-displacement curves measured from extremely
fine grains regions are displayed Fig. S2. For the as-SMGT samples with grain sizes
larger than
4 0 \mathrm { n m }
, Vickers hardness measurements were conducted on a Qness
\mathrm { Q 1 0 A + }
automatic hardness tester with a load of
2 5 \mathrm { g }
and a holding time of 10 s. Meanwhile,
nanohardness of grains with sizes range from
4 0 \mathrm { n m }
to
2 0 0 \mathrm { n m }
are also measured for
comparison, as seen in Fig. S2. The nanohardness values depicted in Fig. 3 were
averaged with at least 10 indentations.
4. Thermal stability measurement
Thermal stability of the nanograined Cu samples was characterized by means of
isothermal annealing. The samples were sealed in vacuum tubes together with small
pieces of pure titanium for preventing oxidation during annealing. The sealed samples
were annealed in a tube furnace with protecting Ar atmosphere and a temperature control
accuracy of
\pm 2 \mathrm { \textrm { K } }
The as-prepared samples were heated rapidly (about
1 0 0 \mathrm { K / m i n } ,
) to different preset
temperatures, holding for 15 min and cooling down for structure characterization. The
annealing temperatures in present work are: 523, 573, 673, 773, 873, 973, 1073, 1173,
1223, 1273, 1318, and 1348 K, respectively. Figure S3 showed the typical bright field
TEM images of nanograins with an initial grain size of about
1 0 \mathrm { n m }
in the as-prepared
sample and as-annealed (at 1173 K, 1273 K, 1348 K) samples, as well as their
corresponding statistical grain size distributions.
5. Construction of 3D CTB networks
The original solution of the "Kelvin problem" consists only two truncated-octahedra
(tetrakaidecahedra) of equal size, the centers of which (grains K1 and K2, Fig. 4A) form
a body centered cubic (BCC) array filling the space (36). Depending on the lattice
orientations of K1 and K2, four pairs of GBs can be formed, with GB planes
perpendicular to each {111} orientations of the supercell. However, no GB can be formed
in any {001} direction in such a way.
An extended Kelvin construction was used to render all surfaces of individual
truncated-octahedra into grain boundaries, which involves sixteen equal-sized
tetrakaidecahedra packing into four layers (Fig. S4), where eight of them are packed first
to fill up the upper-half of the box and then, duplicated and shifted by half the box length
in X, Y and Z directions, respectively, to fill up the lower-half of the box.
The constructed GB network depends on lattice orientations of individual grains and
the way to combine them. Taken polyhedron O1 with cubic orientations of a FCC lattice
as the center cell (Fig. S4), the lattice orientations of its eight nearest neighboring
polyhedra, i.e. A1, B1, C1, D1 (upper panel) and A2, B2, C2, D2 (lower panel), must be
chosen to be in a mirror-reflected pattern such that in each
orientation a Σ3-type
CTB can be formed. According to this rule,
-type lattice orientations can be
specified to the NN-polyhedra (29, 37).
With respect to O1, the assignment of lattice orientations to its second nearest
neighboring (2nd-NN) polyhedra (i.e. I1, E1 and F1 sitting at the same layer) is not
strongly limited. Here, the Σ9-type GBs are considered for all 2nd-NN polyhedral. In
particular, their orientations are chosen such that in each
{ \mathrm { < } } 0 0 1 { \mathrm { > } }
orientation (i.e. X, Y and
Z directions of the supercell) of O1 or O2, a symmetric tilt Σ9 GB is formed. In addition,
it is guaranteed that GBs between any pairs of O1 or O2’s neighboring polyhedra are also
Σ9-type GBs. Detailed lattice orientations of individual grains within the extended Kelvin
box were given in Table S2.
In the as-constructed specimen, the number of grains (N), grain faces (F), grain
edges (E), and grain corners (C), equals to 16, 112, 192 and 96, respectively. They obey
the Euler’s rules
\mathrm { ( N + E = F + C }
\mathrm { N + C = F }
and
\mathrm { E } { = } 2 \mathrm { C }
) with a network connectivity
\mathrm { F } { \cdot } \mathrm { N } { + } 1
or
\mathrm { E - C + 1 }
) of 97 according to the network law (28). Grain edges are triple lines (TLs) and
each TL is formed by three grains. Grain corners or nodes of the GB-network are
quadrupolar points (QPs), where four TLs meet at each QP, as in Fig. S4A.
There is no limit on the size of the polyhedra as long as the enclosed GB portion can
be well-defined down to the atomic scale. A sample of Cu is constructed with an
elemental grain size (the height of a truncated-octahedron in
directions),
\scriptstyle \mathrm { D = } 9 \mathrm { a _ { 0 } }
or
3 . 2 7 \mathrm { n m }
(a0, the lattice parameter), comparable to those in experiments. The supercell
contains 22918 atoms (Fig. S4B). As specified, a space-filling 3D CTB network is
constructed as a sub-GB network. If looking outward within O1 or O2, one found that in
each
direction, only CTBs show up periodically from one cell to the next (Fig.
S4B). That is, four separate sets of intertwined channels are formed. This topological
feature serves to reveal the geometry of polycrystals with high density of CTBs behind
random and seemingly-complicated manifold.
6. MD simulations and visualizations
The thermal stability is assessed via thermal relaxations in NPT-MD simulations. In
particular, we follow a heating procedure to approach a target temperature at a heating
rate of
8 ~ \mathrm { K / n s }
during which the temperature is elevated at an interval of 1 K. Freezing is
carried out as a reverse procedure.
In uniaxial tensile loading test, a tension strain is applied homogeneously at a
constant rate in X, Y or Z direction of the MD supercell during which the system volume
is kept constant. With our MD sample, tensile loading in X, Y or Z-direction,
respectively, are found to produce similar results. Three sets of strain rate
and
1 0 ^ { 7 } / \mathrm { s } \mathrm { \dot { \Omega } }
) were chosen to examine rate effects.
In both the heating and loading procedures, atomic positions and velocities are
integrated and updated each time-step (2.5 fs) on Nose-Hoover canonical style to
maintain a constant temperature. The interatomic potential is based on the semi-empirical
model developed with the embedded atom method (EAM), a reliable classical atomistic
model for simulations of crystal defects in Cu (30). With common neighbor analyses
(CNA), atoms at stacking faults, twin boundaries and other defects can be separated from
those at FCC lattice sites. Grain boundaries can be visualized as a portion of continues
interface using adequate means such as dislocation analyses (DXA) in combination with
planar defect meshing technique implemented in atomic analyses and visualization
software called Ovito (38, 39). All simulations were carried out using the MD package
LAMMPS (40).
Supplementary Text
1. Transformation from Kelvin to Schwarz crystal
The nanograins represented by extended Kelvin polyhedra undergoes significant
structural relaxations upon heating, as shown in Fig. S5. For
\mathrm { T } < 6 4 0 \mathrm { K }
, relaxations of
GB atoms occur strongly, in particular, those at corners (QPs) and edges (triple junction
lines of GBs) of grains. GB reactions (29) may occur at low temperatures
( \mathrm { T } < 4 0 0 \ : \mathrm { K } )
or
even merely via energy minimizations at zero temperature. Comparing to the initial
structure, enhanced geometry complications can be observed. At the temperatures above
400 K, GB atoms became more mobile and diffusive. Fractions of GB atoms and CTB
atoms fluctuate strongly with rising the temperatures to
Enormous atomic events occur during
6 4 0 \mathrm { K } { < } \mathrm { T } { < } 7 3 0 \mathrm { K }
. GB atoms became highly
mobile and diffusive. Migrations of both GBs and CTBs are observed. Grains associated
with the intertwined channels (O1 and O2) expanded at the expense of other grains and
developed into a pair of twinned tetrahedra. Twinning is accomplished via Shockley
partials activated mainly at the nodes of the GB network. Most grains were coalesced,
through which the original GB-network disappeared but the CTB-network remains.
Transformation of GBs into the Schwarz D-surface form (32) is observed at about 750 K.
The transition temperature may vary depending on the heating rates, the size of Kelvin
grains, and other factors such as the orientations of the second neighboring grains (those
not bounded completely by CTBs). For example, for very tiny Kelvin grains (size below
4 \mathrm { n m } ,
), higher heating above 8 K/ns facilitates the transformation within a narrow
temperature window from
6 0 0 \mathrm { K }
to 750 K. For larger Kelvin grains (size about 6 nm), the
transition may occur at lower heating rates, but also at a higher transformation
temperature close to the melting point. For even larger grains (size about
1 0 \mathrm { n m }
), the
transition became sluggish and incomplete.
The areas of GB and CTB in the Schwarz D-structure can be estimated more
precisely in terms of the averaged number density per unit area of grain boundary, which
is
0 . 1 7 4 \mathrm { ~ } / \mathring { \mathrm { A } } ^ { 2 }
for CTB atoms
\left( \rho _ { \mathrm { C T B } } \right)
and
for GB atoms (ρGB) quantified via
separate MD calculations. Taken that GB atoms and CTB atoms are
and
respectively in the Schwarz crystal, the area of Schwarz D-surface (ASD) is about 8738
\mathring { \mathbf { A } } 2
, almost
of the total area covered by the CTBs (ACTB) of the twinned tetrahedra
\mathring { \mathrm { A } } ^ { 2 }
). For our MD cubic cell with a size
\mathrm { L } = 6 5 . 3 \mathrm { 8 } \mathring \mathrm { A }
, the ASD is nearly 2.04 times
the cubic area of the box
( \mathbf { L } ^ { 2 } )
, agrees with Mackay (33) and Schoen (41) who predicted
numerically that ASD is about
Also noted that for our initial sample containing only regular Kelvin
tetrakaidecahedra (unrelaxed), the total surface area shared by the 16 grains in the cubic
box (the same size,
6 5 . 3 8 \mathrm { \AA }
is
, among which
5 5 5 \Dot { 3 } \ \mathring { \mathrm { A } } ^ { 2 }
is occupied by CTBs.
One can see that, after relaxation and the transformation, the total GB area (23070 Å2)
has been decreased by
. The area of CTB, on the other hand, has been more than
doubled (from 5553
\bar { \mathrm { \AA } } ^ { 2 }
to
1 3 0 2 9 \mathring { \mathbf { A } } ^ { 2 \cdot }
), mostly due to GB reactions before the
transformation. The average energy of the GBs assembling the Schwarz D-surface is
estimated to be
5 2 \sim 5 5 \mathrm { m e V } / \mathring { \mathrm { A } } ^ { 2 }
or
8 3 0 \sim 8 8 0 \mathrm { m J } / \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }
at
0 \mathrm { \ K }
, nearly 40 times of the CTB
energy
\mathrm { 1 . 3 7 5 ~ m e V / \mathring { A } ^ { 2 } }
or
2 2 \mathrm { m J } / \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }
As one set of fundamental triply periodic minimal surfaces (a surface of everywhere
vanishing mean curvature) in the mathematical theory, the configuration of Schwarz D-
surface is symmetrically identical to a diamond lattice, which gives the surface its name,
D (32, 41). As such, the nanostructure where GBs can be characterized as the D-surface,
or, any other forms of minimal surfaces in general (41), can be termed as Schwarz
crystal.
2. Thermal stability of Schwarz crystal
The Schwarz crystal demonstrates enhanced thermal stability. As revealed in Fig. S6
A, the nanostructure can survive up to 1322 K, a temperature close to the thermal
equilibrium melting point of Cu,
T _ { m } = 1 3 5 6 ~ \mathrm { K }
. Melting occurs as liquid phase is initiated
at GBs. Neither GB migration nor twinning was observed prior to melting. Without
locking effects of CTB network, GBs can hardly achieve such a superior thermal
stability. This can be shown by examining the mobility of individual GBs, either
symmetric or asymmetric, flat or curved. A few examples are given in Fig. S6 B-D.
Flat GBs are examined using bicrystal supercells built with lattice orientations
specified in Table S2. For example, placing a grain B on top of a grain C will generate a
pair of symmetric Σ9 (22̅1̅) tilt GBs in a MD supercell; while placing B on top of D, on
the other hand, will generate a pair of asymmetric Σ9 (22̅1̅) GBs of both tilt and twist
character (42). It is found that such flat GBs are quite mobile at temperatures below half
of the melting point (Fig. S6 B-C). The CTB is found to be most stable, it remains
immobile and seems capable to sustain a temperature beyond the melting point,
underlying that atoms at CTB is as stable as those in lattice sites (43). Note that the GB
mobility is also found depending on the size of the MD supercell, indicating that
developing significant curvatures via thermal fluctuations to render a GB mobile is more
accessible for smaller MD supercells.
Curved GBs can be examined using the Kelvin bicrystal model. Similar to bicrystal
supercell for flat GBs, there is a large number of choices by referencing the lattice
orientations in Table S2. For example, by choosing two grains, A and F, both of
octahedral shape and of the same size, one can generate a continue portion of asymmetric
and general GBs encapsulated in the MD supercell (Fig. S6 D). It is found that the fully
relaxed GBs now resembles another form of minimal surfaces, the well-known Schwarz
P-surface (32). In this case, the GB migration is apparently diffusion limited (44).
Nevertheless, no thermal stability beyond
seems achievable, as long as the initial
Kelvin cell is no larger than
6 . 5 \mathrm { n m }
in size.
The GB energy is evaluated to be about
7 6 1 \mathrm { m J } / \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }
for the symmetric Σ9 (22̅1̅) tilt
GB. For the asymmetric one, the energy is about
8 7 6 \mathrm { m J } / \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }
3. Mechanical stability of Schwarz crystal
The Schwarz crystal demonstrates enhanced mechanical stability. As demonstrated
in Fig. S7 A, the sample yields at an applied strain close to
. The plastic deformation
can be maintained up to more than
of the applied strain (Fig. S7 B). Tension tests in
X and Y directions or with lower loading rate
( \dot { \varepsilon } { = } 1 0 ^ { 5 } / \mathrm { s } )
) were also carried out, similar
results were obtained. Twinning always constitutes the incipient plasticity events. The
yield stress is best estimated to be 1.8~2 GPa. The elastic constants were also calculated
at
\mathrm { T } = 0 \mathrm { K }
(Table S3), showing that the Schwarz crystal is nearly isotropic. Loading tests
were also carried out at different temperatures. In short, the Schwarz crystal exhibits
enhanced stability against external forces not only at room temperatures but also at high
temperatures close to the melting point.
For comparisons, the tensile loading tests were also carried out for the Kelvin
sample. Note that before loading, the sample were relaxed at temperatures no more than
300 K. To evaluate the structural evolution of the GB network, the fractions of GB atoms
and CTB atoms are extracted and plotted as a function of applied strain (lower panel, Fig.
S7 A). The MD snapshots taken at different stages of loading (Fig. S7 C) showed that the
sample yielded below
of the applied strain at a stress less than 650 MPa. However,
significant hardening effect can be observed with further loading. In particular, when the
applied strain was increased from
to
, the tension stress may exceed 2 GPa,
during which the fraction of GBs has dropped down from
to
. As manifested
by MD snapshots, this occurs as a direct consequence of minimizing the GB area into the
Schwarz D-structure form, a process similar to the structural transformation triggered by
heating. Deformation at higher temperatures facilities the transformation and structurally
more perfect Schwarz crystals can be obtained.
